Signal and System — Complete Guide

9 major topics • Fourier theory, LTI systems, sampling

1. Definition and Concepts

Signal A function of one or more independent variables (like time or space) that carries information about the nature of physical phenomena. Represented as x(t) for continuous-time or x[n] for discrete-time signals.
System A physical device or algorithm that operates on an input signal to produce an output signal — a transformation of input according to specific rules or operations.
Signal Processing The discipline of extracting meaningful information from signals by analyzing, filtering, transforming, and interpreting them using mathematical and computational methods.

2. Classification of Signals

2.1 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals

Continuous-Time Signals

  • Defined for all values of time t
  • Denoted as x(t)
  • Can take any value in a continuous range
  • Example: Audio signal from microphone
  • Amplitude varies smoothly over time

Discrete-Time Signals

  • Defined only at discrete time instances
  • Denoted as x[n], where n is an integer
  • Takes values only at specific intervals
  • Example: Digital audio samples
  • Created by sampling continuous signals

2.2 Deterministic vs Random Signals

Deterministic Signals

  • Behavior is predictable
  • Described by explicit mathematical expression
  • No uncertainty in values
  • Example: x(t) = 5sin(2πt)

Random (Stochastic) Signals

  • Behavior is unpredictable
  • Described using probability functions
  • Cannot be predicted exactly
  • Example: Noise signal, speech signal

2.3 Periodic vs Non-Periodic Signals

Periodic Signal: x(t) = x(t + T) for all t
where T is the fundamental period

Non-Periodic Signal: No such T exists

2.4 Real vs Complex Signals

Property Real Signals Complex Signals
Definition x(t) ∈ ℝ (real numbers) x(t) = xᵣ(t) + j·xᵢ(t)
Range Real number values only Complex plane values
Example Voltage, current, temperature Signal representation in modulation

2.5 Energy and Power Signals

Energy of a Signal:
E = ∫₋∞^∞ |x(t)|² dt   (Continuous-time)
E = Σₙ₌₋∞^∞ |x[n]|²   (Discrete-time)

Power of a Signal:
P = lim(T→∞) (1/2T) ∫₋ₜ^ₜ |x(t)|² dt

Classification:
• Energy Signal: E < ∞ and P = 0
• Power Signal: 0 < P < ∞ and E = ∞
• Neither Energy nor Power Signal: E = ∞ and P = ∞

3. Basic Signal Operations

3.1 Amplitude Operations

Amplitude Scaling

y(t) = A·x(t), where A is a constant

Amplitude Shifting (DC Offset)

y(t) = x(t) + C, where C is a DC component

Amplitude Inversion

y(t) = -x(t)

3.2 Time Operations

Time Shifting

y(t) = x(t - t₀) → Delay by t₀  |  y(t) = x(t + t₀) → Advance by t₀

Time Scaling

y(t) = x(at)  |  |a| > 1: compressed  |  |a| < 1: expanded

Time Reversal

y(t) = x(-t) → Reflection about vertical axis

4. Standard and Elementary Signals

4.1 Unit Step Function

u(t) = { 1, t ≥ 0 }    { 0, t < 0 }

Used for: Switching operations, system response initialization
Properties: Discontinuous at t=0, used as a selector function

4.2 Unit Impulse Function (Dirac Delta)

δ(t) = { ∞, t = 0 }    { 0, t ≠ 0 }

∫₋∞^∞ δ(t) dt = 1

Sifting Property: ∫₋∞^∞ x(t)δ(t − t₀) dt = x(t₀)
Used for: System impulse response analysis

4.3 Ramp Function

r(t) = { t, t ≥ 0 }    { 0, t < 0 }

Relationship: r(t) = ∫ u(t) dt
Used for: Testing system stability and linearity

4.4 Sinusoidal Signals

General Form:
x(t) = A·sin(ωt + φ)  or  x(t) = A·cos(ωt + φ)

where: A = Amplitude  |  ω = Angular frequency (rad/s)  |  φ = Phase (rad)
f = ω/(2π) = Frequency (Hz)  |  T = 1/f = Period (seconds)

Complex Exponential Form:
x(t) = A·e^(jωt) = A[cos(ωt) + j·sin(ωt)]

4.5 Exponential Signals

Real Exponential:
x(t) = A·e^(at), where a is real
• If a > 0: Signal grows exponentially
• If a < 0: Signal decays exponentially

Complex Exponential:
x(t) = A·e^(σ+jω)t = A·e^(σt)·e^(jωt)
σ: Damping factor  |  ω: Frequency of oscillation
• When σ < 0: Damped oscillation  |  When σ = 0: Pure oscillation

5. System Properties and Characteristics

5.1 Linearity

Superposition Principle If y₁(t) = T[x₁(t)] and y₂(t) = T[x₂(t)], then:
T[a·x₁(t) + b·x₂(t)] = a·y₁(t) + b·y₂(t) for any constants a and b

5.2 Time-Invariance

Time-Invariant System A time shift in the input causes the same time shift in the output:
If y(t) = T[x(t)], then y(t − t₀) = T[x(t − t₀)] for any value of t₀

5.3 Causality

Causal System The output at any time t depends only on the input at the present time and past times.
For a causal system: y(t) depends only on x(τ) for τ ≤ t

5.4 Stability (BIBO)

Bounded Input — Bounded Output Stability Every bounded input produces a bounded output.
If |x(t)| ≤ Mₓ < ∞, then |y(t)| ≤ Mᵧ < ∞

Criterion for LTI Systems:
∫₋∞^∞ |h(t)| dt < ∞ (continuous)  |  Σₙ |h[n]| < ∞ (discrete)

5.5 Memory Property

Memoryless System

Output depends only on current input.
Example: y(t) = 2x(t)

System with Memory

Output depends on past/future inputs.
Example: y(t) = x(t) + x(t-1)

6. Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Systems

6.1 Impulse Response

Impulse Response h(t) The output of an LTI system when the input is a unit impulse δ(t).
h(t) = T[δ(t)]

6.2 Convolution

Convolution Integral (Continuous-Time):
y(t) = ∫₋∞^∞ x(τ)·h(t−τ) dτ = x(t) * h(t)

Convolution Sum (Discrete-Time):
y[n] = Σₖ₌₋∞^∞ x[k]·h[n−k] = x[n] * h[n]

Properties of Convolution:
1. Commutative: x * h = h * x
2. Associative: (x * h₁) * h₂ = x * (h₁ * h₂)
3. Distributive: x * (h₁ + h₂) = x * h₁ + x * h₂

6.3 Step Response

Step Response:
s(t) = y(t) when x(t) = u(t)

Relationship with Impulse Response:
s(t) = ∫₋∞^t h(τ) dτ   |   h(t) = ds(t)/dt
Step response shows how system responds to sudden changes

7. Fourier Analysis and Transform

7.1 Fourier Series (Periodic Signals)

Continuous-Time Periodic Signal (Period T):
x(t) = a₀ + Σₙ₌₁^∞ [aₙ·cos(nω₀t) + bₙ·sin(nω₀t)]

where ω₀ = 2π/T (fundamental frequency)

Coefficients:
a₀ = (1/T) ∫₀^T x(t) dt
aₙ = (2/T) ∫₀^T x(t)·cos(nω₀t) dt
bₙ = (2/T) ∫₀^T x(t)·sin(nω₀t) dt

Complex Form:
x(t) = Σₙ₌₋∞^∞ cₙ·e^(jnω₀t)   |   cₙ = (1/T) ∫₀^T x(t)·e^(−jnω₀t) dt

7.2 Fourier Transform (Aperiodic Signals)

Forward Fourier Transform:
X(f) = ∫₋∞^∞ x(t)·e^(−j2πft) dt

Inverse Fourier Transform:
x(t) = ∫₋∞^∞ X(f)·e^(j2πft) df

Angular Frequency Notation:
X(ω) = ∫₋∞^∞ x(t)·e^(−jωt) dt   |   x(t) = (1/2π) ∫₋∞^∞ X(ω)·e^(jωt) dω

7.3 Fourier Transform Properties

PropertyTime DomainFrequency Domain
Linearitya·x₁(t) + b·x₂(t)a·X₁(f) + b·X₂(f)
Time Shiftingx(t − t₀)e^(−j2πft₀)·X(f)
Frequency Shiftinge^(j2πf₀t)·x(t)X(f − f₀)
Time Scalingx(at)(1/|a|)·X(f/a)
Differentiationdx(t)/dtj2πf·X(f)
Integration∫₋∞^t x(τ) dτX(f)/(j2πf) + (X(0)/2)·δ(f)
Convolutionx₁(t) * x₂(t)X₁(f)·X₂(f)
Multiplicationx₁(t)·x₂(t)X₁(f) * X₂(f)
DualityX(t)x(−f)
Parseval's Theorem∫₋∞^∞ |x(t)|² dt∫₋∞^∞ |X(f)|² df

7.4 Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

Forward DTFT: X(e^(jω)) = Σₙ₌₋∞^∞ x[n]·e^(−jωn)

Inverse DTFT: x[n] = (1/2π) ∫₋π^π X(e^(jω))·e^(jωn) dω

Note: X(e^(jω)) is periodic with period 2π

7.5 Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

Forward DFT (N-point):
X[k] = Σₙ₌₀^(N−1) x[n]·e^(−j2πkn/N),   k = 0, 1, ..., N−1

Inverse DFT:
x[n] = (1/N) Σₖ₌₀^(N−1) X[k]·e^(j2πkn/N),   n = 0, 1, ..., N−1

Fast Fourier Transform (FFT):
Efficient DFT algorithm  |  Complexity: O(N log N) vs O(N²)

8. Sampling and Reconstruction

8.1 Sampling Theorem

Nyquist Sampling Theorem A bandlimited signal x(t) with maximum frequency fₘₐₓ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if:
fₛ ≥ 2·fₘₐₓ

The minimum sampling frequency fₛ_min = 2·fₘₐₓ is called the Nyquist Rate

8.2 Sampling Process

Ideal Sampling (Impulse Train):
xₛ(t) = x(t) · Σₙ₌₋∞^∞ δ(t − nTₛ)  where Tₛ = 1/fₛ

Sampled Signal in Frequency Domain:
Xₛ(f) = fₛ · Σₖ₌₋∞^∞ X(f − k·fₛ)

8.3 Aliasing

Aliasing Occurs when fₛ < Nyquist rate. High-frequency components are misrepresented as low-frequency components.

Condition for Aliasing: fₛ < 2·fₘₐₓ
Prevention: Use anti-aliasing filter before sampling

8.4 Reconstruction Filter

Ideal Reconstruction:
x(t) = Σₙ₌₋∞^∞ x[n]·sinc(π(t − nTₛ)/Tₛ)  where sinc(x) = sin(πx)/(πx)

Ideal Low-Pass Filter Cutoff:
fₒ = fₛ/2 = fₘₐₓ (Nyquist frequency)

9. Filters and Filtering

9.1 Filter Types and Characteristics

Low-Pass Filter (LPF)

  • Passes frequencies below cutoff fₒ
  • Attenuates frequencies above fₒ
  • Used for: Noise removal, smoothing
  • Ideal response: Rectangular in frequency

High-Pass Filter (HPF)

  • Passes frequencies above cutoff fₒ
  • Attenuates frequencies below fₒ
  • Used for: DC removal, edge detection
  • Complements low-pass filter

Band-Pass Filter (BPF)

  • Passes frequencies between f₁ and f₂
  • Center frequency: fₒ = √(f₁·f₂)
  • Bandwidth: B = f₂ − f₁
  • Used for: Frequency selection

Band-Stop Filter (Notch)

  • Attenuates frequencies between f₁ and f₂
  • Passes all other frequencies
  • Used for: Noise rejection (60 Hz hum)
  • Complement of band-pass filter

9.2 Filter Design Characteristics

CharacteristicDescriptionSignificance
PassbandFrequency range passed with minimal attenuationContains desired signal components
StopbandFrequency range that should be attenuatedContains unwanted noise/interference
Transition BandRegion between passband and stopbandDetermines filter sharpness
Passband RippleFluctuations in passband gainLess ripple = better signal preservation
Stopband AttenuationGain reduction in stopband (dB)Higher attenuation = better noise suppression
Phase ResponsePhase change across frequencyLinear phase = minimal distortion

9.3 IIR vs FIR Filters

PropertyIIR FilterFIR Filter
Equationy[n] = Σbₖx[n−k] − Σaₖy[n−k]y[n] = Σbₖx[n−k]
Impulse ResponseInfinite lengthFinite length
StabilityCan be unstableAlways stable
Phase ResponseNonlinear (distortion)Can be linear (no distortion)
ComputationLower order for same attenuationHigher order for same attenuation
ImplementationMore efficient (fewer taps)More memory required
Design MethodsBilinear transform, pole/zero placementWindowing, frequency sampling

9.4 Common Filter Types

Butterworth Filter

Maximally flat magnitude in passband. No ripple. Smooth roll-off. Poles on circle in s-plane.

Chebyshev Filter (Type I)

Passband ripple allowed for sharper cutoff. Equiripple in passband. Steeper transition than Butterworth.

Elliptic Filter (Cauer Filter)

Both passband and stopband ripple. Sharpest cutoff among IIR filters. Minimum order for given specs.

Bessel Filter

Maximally flat group delay. Linear phase in passband. Minimal phase distortion. Used for transient preservation.

Analog Communication — Complete Guide

AM, FM, PM modulation • SNR • Noise performance • Pre-emphasis

1. Analog Communication Fundamentals

Analog Communication Transmission of information where the signal varies continuously in both time and amplitude, without any discrete steps or quantization.

1.1 Basic Communication System

Information Source → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver → Destination

• Information Source: Generates message signal m(t)
• Transmitter: Modulates and amplifies signal
• Channel: Medium for signal transmission (air, fiber, cable, etc.)
• Receiver: Demodulates received signal
• Destination: End user receiving the message

1.2 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

Definition: Ratio of signal power to noise power

SNR = P_signal / P_noise

In Decibels:
SNR(dB) = 10·log₁₀(P_signal / P_noise)

For sinusoidal signal x(t) = A·sin(ωt):
P_signal = A²/2

Higher SNR indicates better signal quality

2. Modulation Techniques

Modulation The process of varying the parameters of a carrier wave with the information signal (message signal) to transmit it efficiently over a channel.

2.1 Carrier Wave

General Carrier Wave:
c(t) = Aᶜ·sin(ωᶜt + φᶜ)

where: Aᶜ = Carrier amplitude  |  ωᶜ = Carrier angular frequency  |  φᶜ = Carrier phase

Reasons for Modulation:
1. Antenna size reduction (baseband would need huge antennas)
2. Frequency shifting to avoid interference
3. Efficient use of channel bandwidth
4. Simultaneous transmission of multiple signals (multiplexing)

3. Amplitude Modulation (AM)

3.1 Definition and Equation

Message Signal: m(t) (baseband signal)

Carrier Signal: c(t) = Aᶜ·sin(ωᶜt)

AM Signal:
s(t) = [Aᶜ + m(t)]·sin(ωᶜt)
or equivalently:
s(t) = Aᶜ[1 + μ·mₙ(t)]·sin(ωᶜt)

where μ = Modulation index (0 ≤ μ ≤ 1)  |  mₙ(t) = Normalized message signal (−1 ≤ mₙ(t) ≤ 1)

3.2 Modulation Index and Overmodulation

Modulation Index:
μ = (Aₘₐₓ − Aₘᵢₙ) / (Aₘₐₓ + Aₘᵢₙ)  =  Aₘ / Aᶜ

Classification:
• μ < 1: Under-modulation (poor power utilization)
• μ = 1: 100% modulation (optimal)
• μ > 1: Over-modulation (distortion and splatter)

3.3 Frequency Spectrum of AM

If m(t) = Aₘ·cos(ωₘt):

s(t) = Aᶜ·sin(ωᶜt) + (μAᶜ/2)·sin((ωᶜ + ωₘ)t) + (μAᶜ/2)·sin((ωᶜ − ωₘ)t)

Frequency Components:
• Carrier frequency: ωᶜ (amplitude: Aᶜ)
• Upper sideband: ωᶜ + ωₘ (amplitude: μAᶜ/2)
• Lower sideband: ωᶜ − ωₘ (amplitude: μAᶜ/2)

Bandwidth of AM: BW_AM = 2·fₘ

3.4 Power Distribution in AM

Total Power: Pₜₒₜₐₗ = Pᶜ + P_sidebands

Carrier Power: Pᶜ = Aᶜ²/2

Sideband Power: P_sidebands = (μ²/4)·Pᶜ

Efficiency:
η = P_sidebands / Pₜₒₜₐₗ = μ² / (2 + μ²) × 100%

For μ = 1: η = 33.33% — only 1/3 of power in sidebands (major disadvantage)

3.5 Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)

DSB-SC Signal: s(t) = m(t)·cos(ωᶜt)

Advantages over AM:
1. Carrier is suppressed (no wasted power)
2. Better power efficiency (100% in sidebands)
3. Reduced bandwidth for same information

Demodulation: Multiply by recovered carrier and low-pass filter:
m̂(t) = 2·s(t)·cos(ωᶜt) → LPF → m(t)

3.6 Single Sideband (SSB)

SSB-SC (Suppressed Carrier):

Upper Sideband: s(t) = [m(t)·cos(ωᶜt) − m̂(t)·sin(ωᶜt)]
Lower Sideband: s(t) = [m(t)·cos(ωᶜt) + m̂(t)·sin(ωᶜt)]

where m̂(t) is the Hilbert transform of m(t)

Advantages:
1. Bandwidth only half of AM/DSB (BW = fₘ)
2. More power-efficient than AM
3. Better spectrum utilization

3.7 Vestigial Sideband (VSB)

VSB partially suppresses one sideband while keeping a small vestige.

Bandwidth: BW_VSB ≈ fₘ + fᵥ  where fᵥ is the vestigial frequency

Applications: Television broadcasting (AM-VSB for video)

4. Frequency Modulation (FM)

4.1 Definition and Equation

FM Signal: s(t) = Aᶜ·sin(ωᶜt + φ(t))

Instantaneous Frequency: ωᵢ(t) = ωᶜ + kf·m(t)

Phase Deviation: φ(t) = kf·∫₀ᵗ m(τ) dτ

Maximum Frequency Deviation: Δf = kf·Aₘ

Alternative Form: s(t) = Aᶜ·sin(ωᶜt + Δf/fₘ · sin(2πfₘt))

4.2 Modulation Index (Carson's Rule)

FM Modulation Index: β = Δf / fₘ

Classification:
• β << 1: Narrow-band FM (NBFM)
• β >> 1: Wide-band FM (WBFM)

Carson's Bandwidth Rule:
BW_FM = 2(Δf + fₘ) = 2fₘ(1 + β)

For WBFM (β >> 1): BW_FM ≈ 2·Δf
For NBFM (β << 1): BW_FM ≈ 2·fₘ

4.3 Frequency Spectrum of FM

For single-tone modulation m(t) = Aₘ·cos(ωₘt):

s(t) = Aᶜ·Σₙ₌₋∞^∞ Jₙ(β)·sin((ωᶜ + nωₘ)t)

where Jₙ(β) is the Bessel function of first kind, order n

Important Properties:
1. Infinite sidebands (theoretically)
2. Amplitude depends on Bessel function
3. Sidebands at ±nfₘ where n is integer
4. Jₙ(β) ≈ 0 for n > β + 2 (practical bandwidth limit)

4.4 Power in FM Signals

Total Power (constant): Pₜₒₜₐₗ = Aᶜ²/2

Power Distribution: Pₙ = [Jₙ(β)]²·Aᶜ²/2

Unlike AM, total power is constant and independent of modulation index.

4.5 Advantages of FM over AM

Better noise immunity Constant transmitted power No amplitude distortion Capture effect Better SNR than AM Less affected by fading

4.6 FM Demodulation (Frequency Discriminator)

Principle: Convert frequency variations to amplitude variations

Methods:
1. Slope Detector: Uses frequency-dependent network
2. Balanced Discriminator: Uses tuned circuits
3. Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) Demodulator
4. Quadrature Detector

Transfer Function:
V_out = K·[f(t) − fᶜ] = K·[Δf(t)]

5. Phase Modulation (PM)

5.1 Definition and Equation

PM Signal: s(t) = Aᶜ·sin(ωᶜt + kp·m(t))

where kp is the phase sensitivity constant

Maximum Phase Deviation: Δφ = kp·Aₘ (in radians)

Relationship with FM:
PM is frequency modulation of the derivative of message

5.2 Modulation Index for PM

PM Modulation Index: β = Δφ = kp·Aₘ

Carson's Bandwidth for PM:
BW_PM = 2(Δφ·fₘ + fₘ) = 2fₘ(1 + β)

5.3 PM vs FM Comparison

PropertyFrequency Modulation (FM)Phase Modulation (PM)
Modulating SignalMessage frequency m(t)Message phase kp·m(t)
Frequency DeviationΔf = kf·m(t)Δf = (kp/2π)·m'(t)
Phase DeviationΔφ = kf·∫m(t)dtΔφ = kp·m(t)
Modulation Indexβ = Δf/fₘβ = Δφ
BandwidthBW = 2(Δf + fₘ)BW = 2(Δf + fₘ)
High Freq ResponseIndependent of message frequencyIncreases with message frequency
Noise ImmunityExcellentGood, similar to FM

6. Performance of FM/PM Systems

6.1 Noise Performance (FM)

Output SNR for FM:
SNR_out = (3/2)·β²·SNR_in

Processing Gain:
G = SNR_out / SNR_in = (3/2)·β²

Larger modulation index → better noise immunity

FM Threshold Effect:
Below a certain SNR_in (threshold), performance degrades rapidly

6.2 Pre-emphasis and De-emphasis

Purpose: Improve SNR for high-frequency components

Pre-emphasis Filter (Transmitter):
H_pre(f) = 1 + (f/f₁)²  where f₁ is cutoff frequency

De-emphasis Filter (Receiver):
H_de(f) = 1 / [1 + (f/f₁)²]

Result: SNR improvement of 13–20 dB for audio
📻

Tune the carrier and message signal — watch how amplitude and frequency modulation shape the output waveform live.

BW = 2 Hz Eff = 33%

Digital Communication — Complete Guide

ASK · FSK · PSK · QAM · Channel coding · Shannon capacity · Multiple access

1. Digital Communication Fundamentals

Digital Communication Transmission of information in the form of discrete symbols (bits or higher-level symbols) through a digital modulation scheme and a communication channel.

1.1 Advantages of Digital Communication

Better noise immunity Efficient data compression Error detection/correction Secure encryption Signal regeneration Flexible implementation

1.2 Source Encoding (A/D Conversion)

Sampling: Convert analog signal to discrete samples
Sampling rate fₛ ≥ 2·fₘₐₓ (Nyquist theorem)

Quantization: Convert amplitude to discrete levels
Number of levels: L = 2^B (where B = bits per sample)

Quantization Error:
eₘₐₓ = Δ/2 = V / (2^(B+1))

SNR for Uniform Quantization:
SNR = 3·2^(2B) ≈ 1.76 + 6.02B dB
(Each additional bit improves SNR by ~6 dB)

2. Digital Modulation Techniques

2.1 Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)

Binary ASK Signal:
s(t) = Aᶜ·cos(ωᶜt + φ) for bit 1  |  0 for bit 0

M-ary ASK (M-ASK):
s(t) = Aₘ·cos(ωᶜt)  where Aₘ ∈ {A₁, A₂, ..., Aₘ}

Bandwidth of ASK:
BW_ASK = 2·Rᵦ  (binary)  |  BW_ASK = 2·(Rᵦ / log₂M)  (M-ary)

2.2 Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)

Binary FSK Signal:
s(t) = Aᶜ·cos(ω₁t) for bit 1  |  Aᶜ·cos(ω₀t) for bit 0

Frequency Deviation: Δf = (f₁ − f₀)/2
Center Frequency: fᶜ = (f₁ + f₀)/2

Bandwidth (Carson's Rule):
BW_FSK = f₁ − f₀ + 2·Rᵦ

Coherent FSK

  • Phases aligned between bits
  • Better error performance
  • More complex demodulation
  • Requires phase synchronization

Non-Coherent FSK

  • Phase independent between bits
  • Simpler implementation
  • Slightly worse error performance
  • No phase lock required

Minimum Shift Keying (MSK)

Special case of CPFSK with Δf = Rᵦ/2:

s(t) = Aᶜ·cos(ωᶜt + π/2 · bₖ · t/Tᵦ)

Advantages:
1. Constant envelope
2. Minimum bandwidth among constant envelope schemes
3. Continuous phase (no discontinuities)
4. Better spectral efficiency than standard FSK

2.3 Phase Shift Keying (PSK)

Binary PSK (BPSK)

BPSK Signal: s(t) = bₖ·Aᶜ·cos(ωᶜt)  where bₖ ∈ {±1}

Bandwidth: BW_BPSK = 2·Rᵦ

Advantages over ASK:
1. Constant envelope (constant power)
2. Better noise immunity
3. More efficient for power amplifiers
4. No amplitude distortion problems

Quadrature PSK (QPSK)

QPSK Signal (4-PSK):
s(t) = Aᶜ[I(t)·cos(ωᶜt) − Q(t)·sin(ωᶜt)]

Phase States: φₖ ∈ {π/4, 3π/4, 5π/4, 7π/4}

Bandwidth: BW_QPSK = Rᵦ (half of BPSK!)

Bit Rate vs Symbol Rate: Rᵦ = 2·Rₛ

Advantages: Twice bandwidth efficiency of BPSK. Constant envelope. I-Q implementation.

M-ary PSK (M-PSK)

M-ary PSK Signal:
s(t) = Aᶜ·cos(ωᶜt + 2πk/M),  where k = 0, 1, ..., M−1

Bandwidth Efficiency: BW = Rᵦ / log₂M = Rₛ

Common Forms: 8-PSK, 16-PSK, 32-PSK, 64-PSK

2.4 Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)

QAM Signal:
s(t) = Aᵢ(t)·cos(ωᶜt) + A_Q(t)·sin(ωᶜt)

Complex Notation:
s(t) = Re[S(t)·e^(jωᶜt)]  where S(t) = Aᵢ(t) + j·A_Q(t)

Bandwidth: BW_QAM = Rᵦ / log₂M

Rectangular QAM:
Aᵢ, A_Q ∈ {±1, ±3, ±5, ..., ±(√M − 1)}

Common Types: 4-QAM, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM

3. Digital Modulation Performance

3.1 Error Probability

BPSK Error Probability:
Pᵦ = Q(√(2·Eᵦ/N₀))

where Eᵦ = bit energy, N₀ = noise spectral density
Q(x) = (1/√(2π))∫ₓ^∞ e^(−t²/2) dt

QPSK Error Probability:
Pᵦ = Q(√(2·Eᵦ/N₀))  (same as BPSK!)

For Gray-coded M-PSK:
Pᵦ ≈ Pₛ / log₂M

3.2 Energy per Bit and Power

Energy per Bit: Eᵦ = Pₐᵥₑ · Tᵦ

Signal-to-Noise Ratio:
SNR = Eᵦ · Rᵦ / N₀

3.3 Modulation Comparison

Modulation Bandwidth (for Rᵦ) Pᵦ at SNR=10dB Power Efficiency Envelope
BPSK2·Rᵦ~10⁻⁶HighConstant
QPSKRᵦ~10⁻⁶HighConstant
8-PSKRᵦ/3~10⁻⁴MediumConstant
16-QAMRᵦ/4~10⁻³MediumVariable
64-QAMRᵦ/6~10⁻²LowVariable
FSK2·Rᵦ + BW_dev~10⁻⁵MediumConstant

4. Channel Coding and Error Control

4.1 Error Detection Codes

Parity Check (Even Parity):
Add 1 bit such that total number of 1's is even
Can detect single bit errors, cannot correct

Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC):
Polynomial division method. Can detect multiple bit errors.
Example: CRC-32 uses 32-bit polynomial

4.2 Hamming Codes

Hamming Distance: Minimum number of bit positions differing between two codewords

Error Correction Capability:
To correct t errors: minimum distance d ≥ 2t + 1
To detect t errors: minimum distance d ≥ t + 1

Hamming [7,4] Code:
7 total bits, 4 data bits, 3 parity bits
Can correct 1 error. Code rate R = 4/7 ≈ 0.571

4.3 Convolutional Codes

Constraint Length K: Number of bit periods encoder "remembers"

Code Rate: R = k/n (Typical: R = 1/2)

Viterbi Algorithm:
Optimal soft-decision decoding. Complexity: O(2^(K−1))

4.4 Turbo Codes and LDPC Codes

Turbo Codes:
Parallel concatenation of convolutional codes with interleaving.
Performance near Shannon limit. Used in 3G/4G.

Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC):
Linear block codes with sparse parity-check matrix.
Used in WiFi (802.11), DVB-S2. Lower complexity than Turbo.

5. Digital Transmission Over Channels

5.1 Channel Models

AWGN Channel

  • Additive White Gaussian Noise
  • Most common theoretical model
  • Flat frequency response
  • Linear, time-invariant

Rayleigh Fading

  • Non-line-of-sight (NLOS)
  • Multiple scattering paths
  • Rayleigh distributed amplitude
  • Uniform distributed phase

Rician Fading

  • Line-of-sight (LOS) + NLOS
  • K-factor measures LOS strength
  • More realistic for cellular
  • Rician distributed amplitude

Multipath Fading

  • Multiple propagation paths
  • Frequency selective
  • Time selective (Doppler)
  • Causes ISI (Inter-Symbol Interference)

5.2 Shannon Capacity

Shannon Channel Capacity:
C = B·log₂(1 + SNR)  [bits/second]

where B = Bandwidth (Hz)  |  SNR = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear)

Example:
Voice channel (3.4 kHz, SNR = 10 dB ≈ 10)
C = 3400·log₂(1 + 10) ≈ 11.3 kbps

Power-Limited: C ≈ (SNR/ln2)·B  |  Bandwidth-Limited: C ≈ B·log₂(SNR)

5.3 Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)

Cause: Channel dispersion causes symbol spread

Nyquist Criterion for Zero ISI:
h(nTₛ) = { 1, n = 0 } | { 0, n ≠ 0 }

Raised Cosine Filter:
Roll-off factor α (0 ≤ α ≤ 1)  |  Bandwidth = Rₛ·(1 + α)

5.4 Equalization

Linear Equalization: Equalizer = 1 / Channel_Response

Decision-Feedback Equalization (DFE):
Feedforward filter + feedback filter. Better performance.

Maximum Likelihood Sequence Detection (MLSD):
Viterbi algorithm. Optimal detection.

6. Multiple Access and Multiplexing

6.1 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)

Principle Multiple signals transmitted on different frequencies. Each signal occupies a different frequency band with guard bands preventing interference.

Applications: AM/FM broadcasting, telephone systems, analog cable TV
OFDM: Subcarriers are orthogonal. Higher spectral efficiency. Used in WiFi, LTE, 5G.

6.2 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)

Principle Multiple signals share same frequency, different time slots. Transmissions are interleaved in time and must be synchronized.

Slot Duration: T_slot = Time_per_user × Number_of_users
Applications: Digital telephone systems (GSM, TDMA), data networks

6.3 Code Division Multiplexing (CDMA)

Principle Multiple signals encoded with unique codes, transmitted simultaneously on the same frequency.

Processing Gain: G = L_c / L_b
Spreading Factor: SF = Chip_rate / Bit_rate
Applications: CDMA (3G cellular), WCDMA, DS-CDMA

Comparative Analysis

Side-by-side comparisons of modulation schemes, coding techniques, and system types

1. AM vs FM vs PM Comparison

PropertyAMFMPM
Parameter VariedAmplitudeFrequencyPhase
Mathematical Forms(t) = [Ac + m(t)]sin(ωct)s(t) = Ac·sin(ωct + Δω·∫m(t)dt)s(t) = Ac·sin(ωct + kp·m(t))
Bandwidth2·fm2(Δf + fm)2(Δφ·fm + fm)
Power Efficiency33.3% (at 100% mod.)Constant, good distributionSimilar to FM
EnvelopeVariableConstantConstant
Noise ImmunityPoorExcellentGood
DemodulationSimple (envelope detector)Complex (discriminator)Moderate
ApplicationsBroadcasting, audioBroadcasting, mobile radioSpecialized
SNR ImprovementNone3/2 · β² times inputSimilar to FM

2. Analog vs Digital Communication

AspectAnalog CommunicationDigital Communication
Signal TypeContinuous in time and amplitudeDiscrete symbols (bits/symbols)
Information RepresentationInstantaneous amplitude variesSequence of discrete levels
Channel RequirementsRequires linear channel responseCan tolerate nonlinearity
Noise ImmunityNoise directly degrades signalCan regenerate signal if noise below threshold
Error CorrectionNot possiblePossible with coding schemes
EncryptionDifficult to implementStandard practice
Bandwidth EfficiencyLower efficiencyHigher efficiency
MultiplexingFDM primarilyFDM, TDM, CDMA, OFDMA
Long DistanceRepeaters degrade signalRegenerative repeaters improve signal
ApplicationsOlder radio/TV, specializedModern communications, internet, cellular

ModulationBits/SymbolSpectral Eff.
BPSK10.5 bps/Hz
QPSK21.0 bps/Hz
8-PSK31.5 bps/Hz
16-QAM42.0 bps/Hz
64-QAM63.0 bps/Hz
ModulationConstellation TypeEnvelope
ASKLinear (amplitude)Variable
FSKLinear (frequency)Constant
PSKCircularConstant
QPSKRectangular (4 points)Constant
QAMRectangularVariable

4. Error Coding Techniques Comparison

Code Type Detection/Correction Complexity Efficiency Applications
Parity CheckDetect single bitVery LowLowMemory, communication
HammingCorrect 1 bitLowModerateData storage
ConvolutionalCorrect multiple bitsModerateGoodSatellite, cellular
Turbo CodesCorrect multiple bitsHighExcellent3G/4G cellular
LDPC CodesCorrect multiple bitsModerateExcellentWiFi, satellite
